content:
# Judges Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.3 — "thorns in your sides" vs. "a constant temptation to you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "thorns in your sides" | l'tziddim — "for sides/flanks" |
| LOGOS | "thorns in your sides" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "They will be thorns in your sides, and their gods will be a constant temptation to you" | Adds "constant" to "temptation"; "temptation" for moqesh (snare) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Two issues: (1) moqesh means "snare/trap" — a physical hunting device — not "temptation" (an internal psychological state). The metaphor is external entrapment, not internal desire. (2) PNEUMA adds "constant," which is not in the text. The snare is dangerous precisely because it is not always visible — "constant temptation" implies continuous awareness, while a "snare" implies hidden danger.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Snare ≠ temptation.

---

### v.14 — "he sold them into the hand of their enemies" vs. "he turned them over to their enemies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he sold them into the hand of their enemies" | vayyimk'rem b'yad oy'veihem |
| LOGOS | "he sold them into the hand of their surrounding enemies" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "He turned them over to their enemies all around" | "Turned them over" for "sold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew verb מכר (makhar) means "to sell" — a commercial metaphor. God "sold" Israel to their enemies, as property is sold to a buyer. PNEUMA's "turned them over" loses the economic metaphor. The selling language appears throughout Judges (3:8, 4:2, 10:7) and creates a bitter irony: Israel, who was redeemed (bought back) from Egypt, is now sold to new masters because of their unfaithfulness.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. The "selling" metaphor connects to the redemption/purchase theology of the Exodus.

---

### v.17 — "whored after other gods" vs. "prostituted themselves by worshiping other gods"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they whored after other gods" | zanah acharei elohim acherim |
| LOGOS | "they whored after other gods" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "prostituted themselves by worshiping other gods" | Adds "by worshiping"; passive "prostituted themselves" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew zanah acharei means "to whore after" — active, not passive. PNEUMA's "prostituted themselves by worshiping" adds an explanatory gloss ("by worshiping") and makes the action reflexive-passive rather than active. The author uses the raw verb deliberately: Israel actively pursued other gods, as a person actively seeks illicit partners. The sexual metaphor is meant to shock.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Active "whored after" vs. passive "prostituted themselves."

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA changes metaphor type | 1 | "Temptation" for "snare" (v.3) |
| PNEUMA loses economic metaphor | 1 | "Turned over" for "sold" (v.14) |
| PNEUMA softens sexual metaphor | 1 | "Prostituted themselves" for "whored after" (v.17) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2's disagreements center on metaphor. The Hebrew Bible uses vivid, often disturbing metaphors — selling, whoring, snaring — and PNEUMA consistently softens or abstracts them. The most significant is v.14: the "selling" metaphor connects directly to the Exodus redemption theology. If God "bought" Israel from Egypt (redemption = purchase), then "selling" them back to enemies reverses the Exodus. PNEUMA's "turned over" loses this economic-theological connection.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
